claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 8 years old and does not live with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 8 (under 16)
  - Claimant contributes £26.05/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 8 (under 16); Claimant contributes £26.05/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Claimant contributes £26.05/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you a few questions about your circumstances.

First, do you have any children you're responsible for, and if so, how many children are you asking about?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I have one child - my son Alex. He's 8 years old. I'd like to know if I can claim Child Benefit for him.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! I can see you have one child, Alex, who is 8 years old.

To help determine your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex, I need to ask you a few questions:

Do you currently live in the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great, thank you. 

Does Alex live with you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he doesn't live with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for letting me know. Since Alex doesn't live with you, I need to understand your situation a bit more.

Do you contribute financially towards Alex's upkeep (such as for food, clothes, pocket money, or accommodation), and if so, how much do you contribute?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do contribute towards his upkeep. I help with things like clothes, school dinners, and other essentials. I give about £26 a week.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing that information. That's helpful to know.

Can you tell me who Alex currently lives with? (For example, does he live with his other parent, a relative, or someone else?)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: He lives with his mum, my ex-partner.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. One more important question:

Is Alex's mum currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, she's not claiming Child Benefit for him.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good news! **You are eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex.**

Here's why:

- Alex is 8 years old, which is under the age limit of 16
- You live in the UK
- Even though Alex doesn't live with you, you're contributing approximately £26 per week towards his upkeep (clothes, school dinners, and other essentials)
- Your contribution meets or exceeds the Child Benefit amount (approximately £24 per week for an eldest/only child), which means you're considered responsible for Alex under the rules
- Alex's mother is not currently claiming Child Benefit for him, and only one person can claim for each child

Since you meet all the requirements, you can proceed to make a claim for Child Benefit for Alex. Just remember that if you or your partner have an individual income over £60,000, you may need to pay the High Income Child Benefit tax charge.

Is there anything else you'd like to know about claiming Child Benefit?

reasonings: Alex is 8 years old (under 16), so he meets the age requirement. The claimant lives in the UK. Although Alex doesn't live with the claimant, the claimant contributes approximately £26 per week towards Alex's upkeep (clothes, school dinners, and other essentials). According to the rules, you can still be considered responsible for a child if you're paying at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or the equivalent in kind) towards looking after them. The current Child Benefit rates are approximately £24 per week for the eldest/only child, and the claimant is contributing £26 per week, which meets or exceeds this threshold. Additionally, Alex's mother is not currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex, and only one person can claim Child Benefit for each child. Therefore, the claimant is eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child, Alex (age 8), who lives with his mother. The claimant lives in the UK and contributes approximately £26 per week towards Alex's upkeep, which meets or exceeds the Child Benefit amount. Since Alex's mother is not claiming Child Benefit, and the claimant meets the responsibility test through financial contributions, the claimant is eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex.

Although Alex doesn't live with the claimant, the claimant contributes approximately £26 per week towards Alex's upkeep (clothes, school dinners, and other essentials). According to the rules, you can still be considered responsible for a child if you're paying at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or the equivalent in kind) towards looking after them. The current Child Benefit rates are approximately £24 per week for the eldest/only child, and the claimant is contributing £26 per week, which meets or exceeds this threshold. Additionally, Alex's mother is not currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex, and only one person can claim Child Benefit for each child. Therefore, the claimant is eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child, Alex (age 8), who lives with his mother. The claimant lives in the UK and contributes approximately £26 per week towards Alex's upkeep, which meets or exceeds the Child Benefit amount. Since Alex's mother is not claiming Child Benefit, and the claimant meets the responsibility test through financial contributions, the claimant is eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex.
